critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed with proper temporal splitting, leakage prevention, and appropriate metric choices. I'll focus on the genuine structural issues I found.

**Issue 1: Agent history features create a high-risk leakage pathway**

The methodology assigns agent history features using 'the assigned agent on each ticket,' including that agent's historical distribution of resolution categories. The justification is that in the operational workflow, an owning agent has already been attached at routing time.

The specific claim: at inference time, the owning agent is known before the routing label is assigned, making this a legitimate feature.

Why this might be wrong: 'Resolution category' is the target variable. Computing an agent's historical resolution distribution requires knowing the resolution labels of their prior tickets. If those prior tickets include any tickets from the same temporal window as the training or validation set, this is not leakage in the traditional sense. However, the critical question is how the agent history is computed for the validation and test sets. If the agent's resolution distribution is computed using tickets from months 1–24 only (frozen at training cutoff), that is legitimate but will be stale for agents who change specialization. If it is computed using all tickets up to (but not including) the current ticket, that requires a time-aware feature computation pipeline that is not described. If it is computed using all available data (including test-period tickets), that is direct target leakage. The methodology says these features are 'scaled to the training-set distribution' but does not specify the temporal window used to compute the per-agent resolution distributions. This is a gap, not just an implementation detail — the feature definition itself is ambiguous about temporal scope.

What would constitute evidence: Compute agent history features using only tickets strictly prior to each ticket's timestamp. Compare model performance with this correctly time-windowed version against a version computed using a static training-period window. A significant performance drop in the time-windowed version indicates the static version was implicitly using future information. Separately, check whether agent history features for test-set tickets include resolution data from months 25–30 (the validation period).

**Issue 2: The ablation design does not isolate the stated hypothesis**

The ablation removes metadata (account tier, product line, agent history) together and checks whether text alone still performs similarly. The stated hypothesis is that improvement comes from 'deeper issue understanding' from conversation threads.

The specific claim: if removing metadata causes a large drop, the model relied on shortcuts; if the drop is small, conversation content is the primary driver.

Why this might be wrong: The baseline already uses only text (TF-IDF on the initial message). The proposed model uses text plus all three feature groups. The ablation of the proposed model without metadata is not the same as the baseline — it still uses the full conversation thread, not just the initial message. So the ablation conflates two distinct questions: (a) does the thread add signal beyond the initial message, and (b) does metadata add signal beyond text? To test whether the improvement over the baseline comes from the thread versus metadata, the design needs a model that uses initial message + metadata (no thread), and a model that uses initial message + thread (no metadata), compared against both the full model and the baseline. The current design cannot distinguish between a model that learned to route from account tier alone versus one that genuinely understood the conversation.

What would constitute evidence: Run four models — (1) initial message only (current baseline), (2) initial message + metadata, (3) initial message + thread, (4) all features. The pattern of macro-F1 across these four conditions would isolate the contribution of each component and directly test the stated hypothesis.

**Issue 3: Macro-F1 on a temporally drifting label space may be unstable across splits**

The methodology acknowledges that 'ticket resolution patterns drift with product releases, customer population shifts, and policy updates.' With a strict chronological split, the label distribution in months 31–36 may differ from months 1–24 not just in frequency but in the existence of classes.

The specific claim: macro-F1 is the right primary metric for a multi-class problem with class imbalance.

Why this might be wrong: Macro-F1 averages over all classes equally. If new resolution categories emerge in the test period (months 31–36) that were absent or rare in training, those classes will have zero or near-zero F1, dragging down macro-F1 regardless of model quality. Conversely, if some categories from training become obsolete in the test period, the metric includes them with artificially low support. The methodology excludes only tickets with missing or ambiguous labels but does not address how novel test-period categories are handled. This is not a metric philosophy issue — it is a concrete instability in the denominator of the macro average across the temporal boundary.

What would constitute evidence: Report the number of distinct resolution categories in each temporal split and the overlap between training and test label sets. If more than 5–10% of test-period tickets belong to categories that are new or have very low training-period frequency (e.g., fewer than 20 examples), the macro-F1 comparison between baseline and proposed model is partially measuring memorization of rare training categories versus generalization to novel test categories, which conflates two different phenomena.

These three issues are genuine structural concerns. Issue 1 is the most severe because it involves potential target leakage through an ambiguously specified feature. Issue 2 limits the interpretability of the ablation claim. Issue 3 affects the stability of the primary metric under the temporal drift the methodology itself anticipates.
all_issues_raised: Agent history features (which include historical resolution category distributions) have an ambiguous temporal scope — if computed using tickets beyond the training cutoff, they constitute direct target leakage into validation and test evaluation.

The ablation design (removing all metadata vs. keeping it) cannot isolate whether improvements over the baseline come from conversation thread content versus metadata shortcuts, because it conflates two distinct feature contributions.

Macro-F1 computed over a temporally drifting label space is unstable at the training/test boundary if novel resolution categories emerge in the test period, causing the metric to conflate model quality with label set coverage.
all_issues_adjudicated: Agent history features (which include historical resolution category distributions) have an ambiguous temporal scope — if computed using tickets beyond the training cutoff, they constitute direct target leakage into validation and test evaluation.

The ablation design (removing all metadata vs. keeping it) cannot isolate whether improvements over the baseline come from conversation thread content versus metadata shortcuts, because it conflates two distinct feature contributions.

Macro-F1 computed over a temporally drifting label space is unstable at the training/test boundary if novel resolution categories emerge in the test period, causing the metric to conflate model quality with label set coverage.
